palettes.name: Editorial Off-White
palettes.rationale: Warm off-white background reads as paper, not screen. Oxblood accent gives editorial gravitas without being funereal. Dark variant uses a warmer dark (#0E0E10) and a brighter accent to maintain visual punch.

palettes.name: Newsprint Cream
palettes.rationale: Cream background mimics aged newsprint and reduces the harshness of pure white in long-form reading. Deep forest green is unexpected for editorial and signals craft over trend. Muted brown-gray secondary keeps the page quiet.

palettes.name: Magazine Ivory
palettes.rationale: Ivory base gives a glossy-magazine substrate feel without yellowing. Navy accent reads as authoritative and photographs well in print. The cool muted gray handles captions and metadata without competing with headlines.

palettes.name: Book Paper
palettes.rationale: Antique book-paper white is the gentlest possible background for sustained reading. Burgundy accent references cloth hardcover bindings and leather bookmarks. Sepia-muted secondary ties captions to the substrate.

palettes.name: Linen White
palettes.rationale: Linen tint is warm enough to feel handmade without slipping into yellow. Terracotta accent references unglazed clay and sun-baked earth. The pairing reads artisanal rather than corporate.

palettes.name: Parchment Sepia
palettes.rationale: Parchment background signals historical or archival material before a single word is read. Sepia-brown accent keeps the whole palette in one warm family for cohesion. Aged-paper muted tones avoid modern crispness on purpose.

palettes.name: Broadsheet Gray
palettes.rationale: Newsprint gray base is a deliberate step down from white to evoke a folded morning paper. Cadmium red accent is the only saturated color and earns its place as the single point of visual tension. Everything else is grayscale discipline.

palettes.name: Swiss Red
palettes.rationale: Pure white substrate and a single saturated red is the International Typographic Style distilled to its essence. The red is hot enough to anchor a grid system but never decorative. No gradients, no tints, no apologies.

palettes.name: Swiss Blue
palettes.rationale: White and a single deep blue is the most legible pairing in the Swiss tradition and the most defensible for data-dense UIs. Blue is darkened past the default web-blue to clear AA on white. Muted blue-gray handles secondary labels.

palettes.name: Swiss Magenta
palettes.rationale: Magenta on white is the brasher Swiss cousin — used by Müller-Brockmann-era posters when red felt too political. The accent is pushed dark enough for body links yet reads as electric in display type. Discipline is the point: one color, no tints.

palettes.name: Swiss Mono
palettes.rationale: A palette with no hue at all is a statement: the content is the design. The 'accent' is a darker gray used for emphasis rather than color. Use only when typography and grid are strong enough to carry the page alone.

palettes.name: Helvetica Neutral
palettes.rationale: Off-white base softens the clinical edge of pure white for long SaaS sessions. Burnt-orange accent gives warmth without leaving the Swiss restraint tradition. The whole system is designed to disappear behind content.

palettes.name: Grid System
palettes.rationale: A near-black accent on white forces hierarchy through weight and size, not color. This is the palette for dashboards where data must be read precisely. Color is reserved entirely for semantic state, never decoration.

palettes.name: Brutalist Raw
palettes.rationale: Pure white, pure black, and a single hard red is the brutalist trinity. No rounded corners, no shadows, no sympathy. The red exists only to mark action — everything else is structural.

palettes.name: Brutalist Newsprint
palettes.rationale: Newsprint yellow is the punk-zine base — loud enough to declare intent, pale enough to read. Red and black on yellow is the flyer tradition from xeroxed gig posters. The whole palette is one degree from a photocopy.

palettes.name: Brutalist Mono
palettes.rationale: Black on white with no third color is the most extreme reduction possible. Hierarchy must come from scale, weight, and position alone. Earn this palette only with typography strong enough to survive the absence of color.

palettes.name: Brutalist Terminal
palettes.rationale: Phosphor green on true black is the CRT-terminal palette and a legitimate brutalist statement. The dark variant swaps to amber phosphor — the original monochrome monitor aesthetic. Use only when the technical-reference framing is intentional.

palettes.name: Brutalist Flyer
palettes.rationale: Poster yellow as a full background is the loudest possible opening move. Black-on-yellow inverts to yellow-on-black for the dark variant, preserving the flyer energy. This palette is a declaration, not a backdrop.

palettes.name: Terracotta Desert
palettes.rationale: Cream-tinted base reads as adobe plaster rather than screen. Terracotta accent is the same family as the substrate, giving the palette unity. Earth-toned muted keeps captions from feeling pasted on.

palettes.name: Sage & Stone
palettes.rationale: Pale sage background is borrowed from herbarium plates and Scandinavian interiors. Deep sage accent stays within one hue family for a monochromatic-organic feel. Stone-gray muted handles metadata without introducing a new color.

palettes.name: Ochre Field
palettes.rationale: Ochre on cream references mid-century agricultural posters and harvest imagery. The accent is darkened past raw ochre to clear AA while keeping its mustard character. Muted brown ties captions to the field.

palettes.name: Clay & Cream
palettes.rationale: Two warm earth tones — clay and cream — with no cool counterpoint. The pairing reads handcrafted and small-batch, ideal for maker brands. Muted is a darker cream, never gray, to preserve warmth.

palettes.name: Burnt Sienna
palettes.rationale: Burnt sienna is the painter's earth red, warmer than terracotta and more saturated. On a near-white cream it carries Southwestern and Mediterranean references. The muted tone is a deeper sienna, keeping the palette in one warm arc.

palettes.name: Sandstone
palettes.rationale: Sandstone base is the desert-wall palette — warmer than taupe, cooler than cream. Walnut-brown accent reads as carved detail rather than painted color. The whole system evokes geological strata, not screen pixels.

palettes.name: Olive Grove
palettes.rationale: Olive accent on pale straw is the Mediterranean grove palette. The green is desaturated and warm enough to avoid the 'tech green' trap. Muted olive-gray handles secondary text without breaking the field.

palettes.name: Adobe Wall
palettes.rationale: Adobe is sun-dried clay — warmer than terracotta, softer than brick. The accent is a deeper adobe-red that clears AA on the pale wall. Muted is the shadow side of the same wall, not a separate hue.

palettes.name: Walnut Wood
palettes.rationale: Walnut accent on raw linen is the fine-woodworking palette. The brown is darkened to near-espresso for AA, then warmed in the dark variant. Muted is a tan that reads as unfinished wood.

palettes.name: Emerald City
palettes.rationale: Deep emerald on ivory is the luxury-jeweler palette. The green is darkened past the typical web-green to read as gemstone, not status LED. Cool muted gray-green handles secondary text without warmth creep.

palettes.name: Sapphire Night
palettes.rationale: Sapphire blue is darker and richer than the default web-blue, reading as a gemstone rather than a hyperlink. On warm ivory it carries old-money luxury. Muted slate-blue keeps captions in the cool family.

palettes.name: Ruby Slippers
palettes.rationale: Deep ruby on blush-ivory is the couture palette. The red is darkened into wine territory to clear AA while keeping its jewel character. Muted is a dusty rose-gray, never cold.

palettes.name: Amethyst Glow
palettes.rationale: Amethyst on pale lavender-tinted white is the spiritual-luxury palette. The purple is darkened to clear AA but kept violet, not blue. Muted lavender-gray ties captions to the substrate without a new hue.

palettes.name: Topaz Warmth
palettes.rationale: Topaz amber on warm ivory is the art-deco jewel palette. The accent is darkened past gold-leaf to clear AA while keeping its warmth. Muted bronze handles secondary text within the same warm family.

palettes.name: Garnet Deep
palettes.rationale: Garnet is the deepest jewel red — darker than ruby, warmer than burgundy. On warm ivory it reads as a wax seal. The muted tone is a clay-rose, keeping everything in the warm-red family.

palettes.name: Mint Cloud
palettes.rationale: Mint-tinted background reads fresh without the saccharine edge of candy pastels. The accent is a deep forest mint, darkened past the pastel to clear AA while staying in-family. Use for wellness, food, and lifestyle brands.

palettes.name: Peach Blossom
palettes.rationale: Peach background is the spring-blossom palette — warm without going coral. The accent is a deep clay-coral darkened to clear AA on the pale base. Muted warm-gray keeps captions from competing with the blossom tone.

palettes.name: Lavender Hush
palettes.rationale: Lavender-tinted white is the calmest cool pastel and avoids the cliché lilac-gradient trap. The accent is a deep violet darkened past the pastel for AA. Use for mindfulness, sleep, and women's-health brands.

palettes.name: Powder Blue
palettes.rationale: Powder blue is the safest cool pastel and clears AA easily when the accent is darkened to navy. The pairing reads friendly-professional, ideal for healthtech and education. Muted slate-blue handles secondary labels.

palettes.name: Rose Quartz
palettes.rationale: Rose-quartz background is the softest warm pastel and avoids the bubblegum trap. The accent is a deep rose-red darkened to clear AA. Muted dusty-rose-gray keeps captions in the warm family.

palettes.name: Buttercream
palettes.rationale: Buttercream is the bakery pastel — warm yellow pale enough to read as substrate, not color. The accent is a deep goldenrod darkened to clear AA. Muted olive-gold handles secondary text without cooling the palette.

palettes.name: Pure Mono
palettes.rationale: Pure white and pure black is the maximum-contrast palette and the hardest to design with. Every decision must come from type and space because no color rescues you. The dark variant simply inverts.

palettes.name: Ink on Paper
palettes.rationale: Off-white paper with near-black ink is the reading palette. The accent is a slightly softer black for emphasis that doesn't shout. Warmth in the base prevents the clinical feel of pure white during long sessions.

palettes.name: Charcoal & Snow
palettes.rationale: Cool charcoal on snow-white is the data-UI mono palette. The accent is a dark slate for emphasis within the same family. Use when semantic color must carry all meaning and decoration is forbidden.

palettes.name: Newsprint Mono
palettes.rationale: A step down from white to newsprint gray is the print-publication mono palette. The accent is a darker gray for emphasis, never color. Reserve for editorial brands confident enough to drop hue entirely.

palettes.name: Cinema Noir
palettes.rationale: True near-black with a gold accent is the film-noir and luxury-cinema palette. The gold is the only warmth and reads as title-card lettering. Surfaces are lifted just enough from bg to read as a separate plane.

palettes.name: Film Stock Black
palettes.rationale: Film-stock black is fractionally warmer than pure black to avoid the OLED-crush. Amber accent references physical film leader and sepia prints. Muted blue-gray handles credits-style secondary text.

palettes.name: Teal & Orange
palettes.rationale: Teal-and-orange is the Hollywood color-grade palette and a legitimate cinematic reference. The accent is the orange of graded highlights; teal lives in the substrate. Use only when the cinema reference is intentional, not decorative.

palettes.name: Inception Blue
palettes.rationale: Deep blue substrate with a cold-cyan accent is the Nolan-cinema palette. The blue is dark enough to read as night, not navy. The accent stays cool to preserve the dream-sequence feeling.

palettes.name: Moonlight Blue
palettes.rationale: Moonlight blue is the softest cinematic dark — cold but not black. The pale-blue accent reads as moonlight on a surface. Use for night-mode reading apps and contemplative portfolios.

palettes.name: Drive Neon
palettes.rationale: Magenta neon on near-black is the Refn-Drive palette — retro-cinema, not cyberpunk. The accent is the only saturated color and is reserved for headlines and CTAs. Use sparingly; one neon per page.

palettes.name: Mid-Century Mustard
palettes.rationale: Mustard on cream is the Eames-era mid-century palette. The accent is a saturated mustard darkened just enough for AA on the pale base. Muted olive-gold ties captions to the period.

palettes.name: Mid-Century Teal
palettes.rationale: Teal on warm cream is the mid-century kitchen palette — Heath ceramics, not Miami Vice. The accent is darkened to deep teal for AA. Muted blue-gray handles secondary text within the cool family.

palettes.name: Mid-Century Olive
palettes.rationale: Olive on straw is the mid-century agricultural palette — warm, grounded, anti-glossy. The accent stays muted by design, never brightened. Muted is a darker straw, keeping the whole field warm.

palettes.name: Mid-Century Tomato
palettes.rationale: Tomato-red on cream is the diner-and-diner-menu palette. The accent is darkened past fire-engine red to read as cooked, not raw. Muted warm-brown keeps captions in the kitchen.

palettes.name: Mid-Century Avocado
palettes.rationale: Avocado green on linen is the 1970s kitchen palette, reclaimed without irony. The accent is darkened to a deep avocado for AA. Muted sage-olive handles secondary text in the same family.

palettes.name: Art Deco Gold
palettes.rationale: Black and gold is the art-deco default — Chrysler Building, Great Gatsby title cards. Gold is the only accent and is reserved for rules, headlines, and CTAs. Surfaces lift just enough from true black to read as a panel.

palettes.name: Art Deco Cream
palettes.rationale: The light-mode art-deco palette — cream, gold-leaf, and espresso. The accent is a darkened gold that clears AA on cream. Muted bronze handles secondary text within the warm-metal family.

palettes.name: Art Deco Emerald
palettes.rationale: Emerald and gold is the Great Gatsby party palette — jewel green with metal leaf. The gold accent is the only warm note in a cool-dark substrate. Reserve for luxury hospitality and spirits brands.

palettes.name: Scandi Mist
palettes.rationale: Scandi mist is the palest possible cool gray, borrowed from Nordic interiors. The accent is a desaturated blue-gray, never primary. Muted is a deeper gray for captions without warmth.

palettes.name: Scandi Wood
palettes.rationale: Scandi wood is the birch-and-ash interior palette — warm pale wood on off-white. The accent is a darker walnut for AA. Muted is a tan that reads as unfinished wood, never gray.

palettes.name: Scandi Slate
palettes.rationale: Scandi slate is the cool-gray UI palette — restrained enough for dashboards, warm enough to avoid clinical feel. The accent is a dark slate-blue for AA. Muted blue-gray handles secondary labels within the cool family.

palettes.name: Scandi Clay
palettes.rationale: Scandi clay softens terracotta into a muted rose-brown for Nordic interiors. The accent is darkened clay that clears AA on the pale base. Muted is a deeper clay, keeping the warm field unified.

palettes.name: Scandi Fog
palettes.rationale: Scandi fog is the grayest possible palette with no warm note at all. The accent is a dark blue-gray for emphasis within the family. Use for editorial photography portfolios where color must not compete with imagery.

palettes.name: Code Editor Dark
palettes.rationale: GitHub-dark substrate with a single syntax-blue accent is the developer-familiar palette. The accent matches how devs already read code, lowering cognitive load. Semantic colors double as syntax-highlight roles.

palettes.name: Code Editor Light
palettes.rationale: Light code-editor palette uses GitHub-light conventions so docs feel continuous with the codebase. The accent is the GitHub link-blue, darkened to clear AA. Semantic colors are the same hues devs already parse in diffs.

palettes.name: Terminal Amber
palettes.rationale: Amber-on-black is the original monochrome CRT palette and a legitimate technical reference. The amber is the only color, used for both text and accent at different weights. Use for terminal-style UIs and retro-tech brands.

palettes.name: Blueprint
palettes.rationale: Blueprint is the architect's palette — deep blueprint-blue substrate with white line-work and an amber annotation accent. The amber reads as pencil-on-blueprint. Use for engineering and architecture brands.

palettes.name: Schematic Green
palettes.rationale: Phosphor-green-on-dark-green is the oscilloscope palette. The accent is brighter green for active traces, text is softer green for labels. Use for engineering-tool brands and hardware dashboards.

palettes.name: Forest Floor
palettes.rationale: Forest floor is leaf-mold brown with deep moss-green accent. The pairing reads as woodland, not 'eco-brand green'. Muted is a bark-brown for captions within the warm-earth family.

palettes.name: River Stone
palettes.rationale: River stone is the wet-stone palette — cool gray with a slate-blue accent. The accent reads as water without being 'tech blue'. Muted is a deeper stone-gray for captions.

palettes.name: Meadow
palettes.rationale: Meadow is sunlit grass — warm straw base with a fresh grass-green accent. The green is yellow-leaning to avoid the 'wellness mint' trap. Muted olive handles secondary text in the same field.

palettes.name: Coastal
palettes.rationale: Coastal is overcast seaside — pale gray-blue with a deep teal accent. The accent is darkened sea-blue for AA. Muted slate handles captions without introducing warmth.

palettes.name: Autumn Maple
palettes.rationale: Autumn maple is the October palette — cream substrate with a maple-red accent. The accent is darkened rust for AA. Muted warm-brown ties captions to the forest floor.

palettes.name: Stone Garden
palettes.rationale: Stone garden is the zen-garden palette — warm stone base with a bronze accent. The accent reads as raked sand, not metal. Muted is a deeper stone, keeping everything earthy and quiet.

palettes.name: Retro Diner
palettes.rationale: Retro diner is the chrome-and-vinyl palette — cream, tomato red, and espresso. The red is darkened past fire-engine for AA. Muted brown handles menu-text secondary within the warm family.

palettes.name: Retro 70s Orange
palettes.rationale: 70s orange is the conversation-pit palette — burnt orange on harvest gold. The accent is darkened past pumpkin for AA. Muted walnut handles secondary text in the same warm family.

palettes.name: Retro 80s Miami
palettes.rationale: Miami 80s is the Vice palette — magenta neon on deep purple-black. The accent is the only saturated color and is reserved for headlines. Use only when the retro reference is the concept, not a garnish.

palettes.name: Retro 90s Web
palettes.rationale: 90s web is the Windows 95 palette — teal background, cream text, and a yellow link-accent. The combination is deliberately period-correct. Use only with irony and strong typography.

palettes.name: Retro Polaroid
palettes.rationale: Polaroid is the faded-photo palette — warm cream with a dusty rose accent. The accent is darkened mauve for AA. Muted warm-brown handles caption text in the same faded family.

palettes.name: Luxury Onyx
palettes.rationale: Onyx and gold is the watchmaker palette — true near-black with a single metal accent. The gold is reserved for rules and CTAs, never body. Surfaces lift one shade from bg to read as separate panels.

palettes.name: Luxury Cream & Bronze
palettes.rationale: Cream and bronze is the light-mode luxury palette — stationery, not jewelry. The bronze accent is darkened to clear AA on cream. Muted olive-bronze handles secondary text within the metal family.

palettes.name: Luxury Wine
palettes.rationale: Wine and gold is the Bordeaux palette — deep oxblood substrate with metal-leaf accent. The gold is the only light note in a dark-red field. Use for spirits, fashion, and hospitality brands.

palettes.name: Luxury Pearl
palettes.rationale: Pearl is the softest luxury palette — warm off-white with a bronze accent. The accent is darkened to deep bronze for AA. Muted warm-gray handles captions without cooling the pearl.

palettes.name: Luxury Ink & Gold
palettes.rationale: Ink and gold is the calligrapher's palette — warm-black ink with gold-leaf accent. The black is fractionally warm to avoid the OLED-crush of pure black. Gold is reserved for headlines and rules.

palettes.name: Playful Primary
palettes.rationale: Primary palette is the children's-book palette — cream, ink, and a single bold blue. The blue is darkened past primary-blue for AA. Muted blue-gray handles secondary text within the cool family.

palettes.name: Maximalist Carnival
palettes.rationale: Carnival is the loudest warm palette — cream, red, and ink. The red is the only saturated color and earns its volume by being rare. Muted warm-brown keeps secondary text quiet against the noise.

palettes.name: Maximalist Disco
palettes.rationale: Disco is gold-on-purple, the Studio 54 palette. The gold accent is the only warm note in a cool-dark field. Use only for nightlife, fashion, and event brands where excess is the concept.

palettes.name: Playful Sprout
palettes.rationale: Sprout is the children's-garden palette — pale straw with a fresh leaf-green accent. The green is darkened past grass-green for AA. Muted sage handles secondary text in the same garden.

palettes.name: Playful Bubblegum
palettes.rationale: Bubblegum is the candy palette — pale pink with a deep rose accent. The accent is darkened past bubblegum for AA. Muted dusty-rose handles secondary text within the warm family.

palettes.name: Studio White
palettes.rationale: Studio white is the designer-portfolio palette — pure white with a single deep teal-blue accent. The accent is darkened past cyan for AA. Muted slate handles metadata without introducing a hue.

palettes.name: Studio Bone
palettes.rationale: Studio bone is the warm-minimalist palette — bone white with a single walnut accent. The accent is darkened espresso for AA. Muted warm-gray handles captions within the earth family.

palettes.name: Studio Slate
palettes.rationale: Studio slate is the product-UI palette — cool gray with a dark slate accent. The accent stays within the gray family for hierarchy without hue. Use when semantic color carries all meaning.

palettes.name: Studio Sand
palettes.rationale: Studio sand is the warm-neutral minimalist palette — sand with a deep umber accent. The accent is darkened walnut for AA. Muted tan handles captions in the same earth family.

palettes.name: Studio Ink
palettes.rationale: Studio ink is the dark-minimalist palette — near-black with a single cyan accent. The accent is brightened for dark-mode AA. Use for design-studio sites that want dark without going neon.

palettes.name: Moody Plum
palettes.rationale: Moody plum is the autumn-dusk palette — deep wine substrate with a dusty-rose accent. The accent is brightened for dark-mode AA. Use for fashion and editorial portfolios that want warmth without brightness.

palettes.name: Moody Indigo
palettes.rationale: Moody indigo is the twilight palette — deep indigo substrate with a soft periwinkle accent. The accent is brightened for dark-mode AA. Use for editorial and music brands wanting night without neon.

palettes.name: [BANNED] AI Default Indigo
palettes.rationale: This is the single most overused AI-generated palette: Tailwind slate-50 background with an indigo accent. Even darkened to indigo-700 for AA, the slate-plus-indigo combo instantly reads as 'LLM-generated SaaS landing page'. Forbidden because it is the tell, not because it is broken.

palettes.name: [BANNED] Indigo-Violet-Pink Gradient
palettes.rationale: The indigo-to-violet-to-pink linear gradient is the universal AI-hero default. Represented here as a flat palette for documentation; the gradient itself is the banned artifact. Forbidden because no human designer reaches for it first.

palettes.name: [BANNED] Pure RGB Primaries
palettes.rationale: Pure RGB primaries (#FF0000 / #00FF00 / #0000FF) are the most saturated colors any screen can produce and the loudest possible AI tell. The blue here is darkened to clear AA but the category is banned. Forbidden because real designers modulate saturation; machines max it out.

palettes.name: [BANNED] Material Blue Default
palettes.rationale: Material Design's default blue (#1976D2 / #1565C0) was the 2014 default and remains the second-loudest AI tell after indigo. Forbidden because it carries a decade of generic Android-app baggage. Pick a blue with a deliberate temperature instead.

palettes.name: [BANNED] Glassmorphism Aurora
palettes.rationale: The aurora-blob glassmorphism hero with frosted cards over a pastel-gradient mesh is the dominant AI landing-page aesthetic of 2024-2025. Represented here flat; the gradient-plus-blur is the banned artifact. Forbidden because it signals 'generated' before the user reads a word.
